children.name: Chamaeleo
children.description: The true chameleons of the Old World — masters of active colour change through chromatophores, iridophores, and melanophores arranged in layers within the skin. Contrary to popular belief, chameleons do not change colour primarily for camouflage; colour change communicates social status, temperature, and emotion — a submissive male rapidly fades to pale, while a dominant male blazes with vivid signals. Eyes move independently, providing simultaneous nearly-360° vision. The projectile tongue, up to twice the body length, accelerates at 264 g to strike prey.

children.name: Chamaeleo chamaeleon
children.description: The only chameleon native to Europe, found in Iberia, southern Greece, and the Mediterranean islands. Oviparous; buries eggs in soil. The northernmost-occurring chameleon species.

children.name: Chamaeleo calyptratus
children.description: Native to Yemen and Saudi Arabia. The dramatic casque (helmet) on the head channels morning dew and rainwater into the mouth — an adaptation for an arid environment. The most common chameleon in captivity.

children.name: Chamaeleo dilepis
children.description: The most widespread chameleon in sub-Saharan Africa, occupying savanna woodland from Senegal to Tanzania and south to Mozambique. When threatened, it opens the prominent lateral flaps on its neck to appear larger. One of the species most frequently encountered crossing roads in African savannas.

children.name: Chamaeleo gracilis
children.description: A slender West and Central African chameleon found from Senegal across to Sudan and Uganda. Distinguished from the flap-necked chameleon by its more elongated body and less prominent lateral flaps. Commonly found in shrubs and low trees in dry savanna habitat.

children.name: Chamaeleo namaquensis
children.description: Unique among chameleons for being primarily terrestrial, stalking prey on the ground of the Namib Desert and Namaqualand. It uses colour change for thermoregulation — turning black in the cool morning to absorb heat and pale during the hottest part of the day. Unusually aggressive; adults will cannibalize smaller individuals.

children.name: Chamaeleo senegalensis
children.description: A small chameleon distributed across the savannas and forest-edge habitats of West Africa from Senegal to Cameroon. Viviparous, giving birth to 5–20 live young rather than laying eggs. Heavily collected for the international pet trade, leading to population declines across much of its range.

children.name: Chamaeleo zeylanicus
children.description: The only chameleon found in the Indian subcontinent, occurring in scrub forests and dry areas across India and Sri Lanka. Like the common chameleon, it is oviparous, burying clutches of 10–30 eggs. It is associated in Hindu mythology with Indra and considered a protective omen in some rural traditions.

children.name: Chamaeleo africanus
children.description: Found in Egypt, Libya, and with isolated populations in Greece — making it one of only two chameleons with a confirmed European presence. Inhabits arid scrubland and agricultural areas near rivers and oases. Closely related to the common chameleon but differs in scalation and casque shape.

children.name: Chamaeleo laevigatus
children.description: A smooth-scaled chameleon from Central and East African forests; vivid green with a pale lateral stripe.

children.name: Chamaeleo monachus
children.description: Endemic to Socotra; the only chameleon on the island; adapted to arid bushland.

children.name: Chamaeleo randa
children.description: A species from the Kenyan highlands; males develop a high casque during displays.

children.name: Chamaeleo monachus
children.description: Endemic to the island of Socotra; a large green chameleon with a curved crest.

children.name: Chamaeleo necasi
children.description: A recently described chameleon from the forests of Tanzania.

children.name: Chamaeleo senegalensis
children.description: A widespread West African chameleon; easily identified by the three occipital lobes.

children.name: Chamaeleo temnickii
children.description: A South African species; dark brown with white bands; named after the Dutch zoologist.

children.name: Furcifer
children.description: Malagasy chameleons — a large and spectacular genus endemic almost entirely to Madagascar, a continent-island that is a global hotspot of chameleon diversity, hosting nearly half of all chameleon species. The Panther Chameleon shows extraordinary geographic colour variation: males from different regions ('locales') differ so dramatically in colour that they were long thought to be different species. Females signal receptivity and pregnancy state through distinctive colour changes.

children.name: Furcifer pardalis
children.description: Males show extreme geographic colour variation across Madagascar — red-and-blue from Ambilobe, turquoise-and-green from Nosy Be, orange from Ambanja. A 2016 study confirmed that what were thought to be one species may actually be 11 distinct lineages.

children.name: Furcifer lateralis
children.description: One of the most beautiful chameleons — females show a striking pattern of white spots and turquoise on green, orange, and black depending on state. Found in open grassland and agricultural areas across much of Madagascar.

children.name: Furcifer oustaleti
children.description: One of the largest chameleons in the world, reaching 68 cm. Found across a wide range of habitats in Madagascar. Males are relatively drab — grey-brown — compared to most Furcifer species.

children.name: Furcifer verrucosus
children.description: A large, heavily tuberculated chameleon restricted to the arid southwest and south of Madagascar, where it inhabits spiny forest and dry savanna. Males display a striking white lateral stripe and blue-green flecks on a sandy or olive body. Tolerates significantly drier conditions than most Furcifer species.

children.name: Furcifer labordi
children.description: Remarkable for having the shortest post-hatching lifespan of any tetrapod — adults die within 4–5 months of hatching, spending more time as an egg than as a living animal. Endemic to the seasonally arid deciduous forests of southwestern Madagascar. Named after the French naturalist Jean Labord.

children.name: Furcifer willsii
children.description: A moderately-sized, spectacularly coloured chameleon of the humid rainforest canopy in eastern Madagascar. Males are vivid green with blue and white lateral stripes; females are considerably more muted. It is closely associated with primary humid forest and is threatened by ongoing deforestation.

children.name: Furcifer bifidus
children.description: Distinguished by the prominent bifid (forked) rostral appendage on the snout of males, giving the species its name. Found in humid forests of eastern and northern Madagascar. One of the more striking Malagasy chameleons, displaying vivid green, blue, and yellow colouration.

children.name: Furcifer rhinoceratus
children.description: One of the most endangered chameleons in Madagascar, Critically Endangered due to the near-total destruction of the dry deciduous forests of northwestern Madagascar where it lives. Males bear a pronounced bony rostral horn resembling a rhinoceros horn. Fewer than 10 known locations remain.

children.name: Furcifer balteatus
children.description: A small chameleon from southeastern Madagascar; two light bands cross the dark body.

children.name: Furcifer major
children.description: A larger Furcifer from eastern Madagascar; closely related to F. pardalis but less colourful.

children.name: Furcifer timoni
children.description: A recently described species from the Montagne d'Ambre region of northern Madagascar.

children.name: Furcifer viridis
children.description: A bright green Furcifer from western Madagascar; males have a prominent rostral appendage.

children.name: Furcifer timoni
children.description: A recently described species from the Montagne d'Ambre region.

children.name: Furcifer balteatus
children.description: A medium-sized chameleon from eastern Madagascar; distinct transverse yellow bands.

children.name: Trioceros
children.description: Three-horned chameleons — an African genus famous for the three prominent rostral horns of the males, used in combat. Jackson's Chameleon is native to East Africa but has established a large feral population in Hawaii following the accidental release of captive animals in the 1970s. Meller's Chameleon is the largest chameleon in mainland Africa. Most Trioceros species are viviparous — giving birth to live young rather than laying eggs, an unusual trait among chameleons.

children.name: Trioceros jacksonii
children.description: Named for Sir Frederick John Jackson, colonial administrator and ornithologist. Males bear three horns — one rostral and two preocular — used in shoving contests for territory. The feral Hawaiian population is considered an invasive species.

children.name: Trioceros melleri
children.description: The largest chameleon on mainland Africa, reaching up to 60 cm. A solitary inhabitant of highland East African forest. The small, single rostral horn distinguishes it from the three-horned species.

children.name: Trioceros quadricornis
children.description: Endemic to the montane forests of western Cameroon and eastern Nigeria, where it lives at elevations above 1,500 m. Males bear four prominent horns — two rostral and two preocular — an unusual configuration in the genus. Threatened by forest clearance for agriculture and the bushmeat trade.

children.name: Trioceros johnstoni
children.description: A large, viviparous chameleon of the Albertine Rift montane forests in Rwanda, Uganda, and the Democratic Republic of Congo. Males bear three prominent horns and display vibrant green, blue, and yellow. Named after Sir Harry Johnston, the British colonial administrator and naturalist.

children.name: Trioceros hoehnelii
children.description: Found in the highland moorlands of Kenya and Ethiopia at elevations up to 3,500 m, making it one of the highest-altitude chameleons. Characterised by a prominent high casque on the head. Viviparous, producing 5–20 live young.

children.name: Trioceros cristatus
children.description: A West and Central African forest species recognisable by the prominent dorsal crest of enlarged, blade-like scales running the length of the back. Distributed from Nigeria east to Uganda and south to Angola. Unlike many Trioceros, this species lacks rostral horns but compensates with its distinctive crest.

children.name: Trioceros deremensis
children.description: Restricted to the West and East Usambara Mountains of northeastern Tanzania, one of the world's most important biodiversity hotspots. Males bear three annulated horns and display vivid green with blue flanks. Critically threatened by ongoing deforestation of the Usambara forests for tea and cardamom cultivation.

children.name: Trioceros bitaeniatus
children.description: A small montane chameleon of the highlands of Kenya, Tanzania, Uganda, and Rwanda, identifiable by a prominent pale lateral stripe running the length of the body. Viviparous and live-bearing, producing small litters of 5–15 young. Commonly encountered in highland gardens and forest edges.

children.name: Trioceros oweni
children.description: A West and Central African forest canopy species found from Sierra Leone to Uganda, bearing a single prominent rostral horn. Named after Sir Richard Owen, the British anatomist who described many natural history specimens. Males are strikingly green with blue and white flanking marks.

children.name: Trioceros werneri
children.description: A medium-sized, three-horned chameleon endemic to the Udzungwa and Nguru Mountains of central Tanzania. One of the more poorly known Tanzanian Trioceros species; found in humid montane forest above 1,200 m elevation. Threatened by agricultural encroachment into the montane forest zone.

children.name: Trioceros affinis
children.description: A small highland chameleon from the Ethiopian highlands; three prominent horns in males.

children.name: Trioceros kinetangbae
children.description: A high-altitude specialist from Mount Kenya; vivid turquoise blue in males.

children.name: Brookesia
children.description: Leaf chameleons — tiny, leaf-litter-dwelling chameleons of Madagascar, some of which are the smallest reptiles and tetrapods on Earth. Brookesia nana, described in 2021, is the world's smallest reptile: adult males measure just 13.5 mm from snout to vent. These miniaturised chameleons are thought to be cryptically patterned to mimic dead leaves, and lack the dramatic colour-change ability of larger species. Their tiny size and specialised habitat make them extremely vulnerable to forest clearance.

children.name: Brookesia micra
children.description: Described in 2012 from the islet of Nosy Hara, Madagascar. At the time of description, the smallest chameleon and one of the smallest reptiles in the world. Adults sleep on low vegetation at night, returning to the leaf litter by day.

children.name: Brookesia nana
children.description: Described in 2021 from the Sorata massif of northern Madagascar. The male holotype is 13.5 mm snout–vent — the smallest known adult of any reptile species. The female is almost twice as long, an extreme case of sexual size dimorphism.

children.name: Brookesia superciliaris
children.description: One of the larger Brookesia leaf chameleons, found in primary rainforest of eastern Madagascar. The common name refers to prominent spine-like supraorbital tubercles that project over the eyes. Unlike the smallest Brookesia, this species still retains limited colour-change ability.

children.name: Brookesia perarmata
children.description: The most heavily armoured of all Brookesia species, bearing rows of sharp keeled scales and spines along the back and flanks. Endemic to a small patch of dry deciduous forest near Antsalova in western Madagascar. Listed as Vulnerable due to its extremely restricted range and ongoing habitat loss.

children.name: Brookesia stumpffi
children.description: A moderately-sized Brookesia from northwestern Madagascar, more tolerant of degraded and secondary forest than many relatives. One of the most commonly encountered Brookesia due to its wider habitat tolerance. Named in honour of Ottomar Stumpff, a German naturalist who collected specimens in Madagascar.

children.name: Brookesia ebenaui
children.description: A tiny leaf chameleon from the humid forests of Nosy Be island and the nearby mainland of northwestern Madagascar. Like other Brookesia, it spends the day walking slowly through leaf litter, camouflaged as a dead brown leaf. Named after C. Ebenau, a German colonial official who collected natural history specimens.

children.name: Brookesia minima
children.description: One of the smallest Brookesia species, found in humid forests of Nosy Be and adjacent mainland northwestern Madagascar. Adults reach only 30 mm total length. Its extremely small range and dependence on intact primary forest make it sensitive to habitat disturbance.

children.name: Brookesia branchia
children.description: A tiny leaf chameleon from northern Madagascar; named after Bill Branch.

children.name: Brookesia brygooi
children.description: A small species from the dry forests of western Madagascar; named after the herpetologist.

children.name: Brookesia desperata
children.description: Critically Endangered species from a tiny forest fragment; named for its conservation plight.

children.name: Brookesia minima
children.description: One of the world's smallest reptiles; adults reach only 3 cm total length.

children.name: Brookesia micra
children.description: A recently discovered minute chameleon from Nosy Hara, Madagascar.

children.name: Brookesia perarmatus
children.description: A well-armored leaf chameleon with prominent spines above the eyes.

children.name: Brookesia brunoi
children.description: A recently described species from the Tsingy de Bemaraha in Madagascar.

children.name: Calumma
children.description: Calumma chameleons — a genus of largely arboreal, forest-dwelling chameleons endemic to Madagascar, characterised by elaborate head ornamentation including rostral appendages, casques, and crests. Parson's Chameleon is the largest chameleon in the world by weight and one of the longest-lived chameleons, reaching 10+ years. It is slow-maturing and slow-reproducing, making it particularly sensitive to collection for the pet trade.

children.name: Calumma parsonii
children.description: The heaviest chameleon and one of the longest-lived lizards. Males can reach 68 cm and 700 g. Restricted to humid primary rainforest in eastern Madagascar; highly sensitive to habitat disturbance. Export is now banned.

children.name: Calumma brevicorne
children.description: A medium-sized Calumma with a short, compressed rostral appendage, found in humid montane forests of eastern Madagascar above 900 m. Males are vivid green with blue-white lateral spots; females are brown with a more muted pattern. One of the more frequently encountered Calumma in accessible highland forests.

children.name: Calumma nasutum
children.description: A small, widespread Calumma found throughout the humid forests of eastern Madagascar from sea level to 1,800 m. Bears a flexible, soft rostral appendage. One of the most adaptable Calumma species, occurring in disturbed secondary forest as well as pristine primary habitat.

children.name: Calumma globifer
children.description: One of the largest Calumma species, characterised by its unusually rounded, globe-shaped head casque. Restricted to the humid forests of the Ranomafana area of southeastern Madagascar. Males display intense blue-green colouration with orange flanks during male–male interactions.

children.name: Calumma gastrotaenia
children.description: A small to medium-sized Calumma widespread in the eastern rainforest belt of Madagascar. Named for the distinctive banding on the ventral surface. Highly variable in colour and scalation across its range, leading to recognition of multiple subspecies.

children.name: Calumma gallus
children.description: Named for the rooster-comb-like crest of enlarged scales along the dorsal midline. An arboreal species of eastern Malagasy rainforest, rarely descending to the ground. Males bear a prominent rostral appendage and display vivid green and yellow colouration.

children.name: Calumma tarzan
children.description: Described in 2010 and named informally after the fictional jungle character because it was found in the Tarzanville forest fragment of eastern Madagascar. Known from just a few square kilometres of heavily degraded forest, making it one of the most range-restricted and threatened chameleons in the world.

children.name: Calumma ambiguum
children.description: A small Calumma from eastern Madagascar; males have a short rostral appendage.

children.name: Calumma electricum
children.description: Known for the electric blue colouration of males during courtship; restricted to remaining forests.

children.name: Calumma ohshima
children.description: A recently described species from northwestern Madagascar; named after a Japanese benefactor.

children.name: Calumma tigris
children.description: A spectacular tiger-striped chameleon from northeastern Madagascar.

children.name: Calumma galus
children.description: A recently described chameleon from the Marojejy massif.

children.name: Calumma venosa
children.description: A small species with distinctive blue veins visible through the skin.

children.name: Calumma hilleniusi
children.description: A montane chameleon from the Andringitra massif; green with lateral white bands.

children.name: Calumma jezni
children.description: A recently described species from the Tsaratanana massif.

children.name: Rhampholeon
children.description: African pygmy chameleons — small, leaf-litter and low-shrub-dwelling chameleons of Central and East Africa, analogous to the Malagasy Brookesia in ecological role and body plan. Most are cryptically patterned in shades of brown and grey to mimic dead leaves, bark, or forest floor detritus. Many species are restricted to single mountain ranges or forest blocks, making them extremely vulnerable to deforestation. Spectral chameleons are a key indicator species for forest health across the Albertine Rift and Eastern African mountains.

children.name: Rhampholeon spectrum
children.description: The largest Rhampholeon species, found in rainforests of Cameroon, Equatorial Guinea, Gabon, and the Congo Basin. Despite the name, males show limited colour change — mostly muted browns and greys. Unlike most family members, males of this species fluoresce blue under UV light from bony skull tubercles.

children.name: Rhampholeon temporalis
children.description: Endemic to the East Usambara Mountains of Tanzania, where it inhabits the moist forest floor between 300 and 900 m. Named for the distinctive pits on the temporal scales. A popular chameleon in captivity due to its manageable size and striking pattern of grey, beige, and brown.

children.name: Rhampholeon spinosus
children.description: Found in montane forests of the Uluguru, Nguru, and Udzungwa Mountains of Tanzania, this species is characterised by prominent spine-like dorsal tubercles. A specialist of intact humid forest above 700 m, it is threatened by ongoing clearance of its montane forest habitat.

children.name: Rhampholeon marshalli
children.description: Distributed across the Eastern Highlands of Zimbabwe and adjacent Mozambique, including the Chimanimani and Vumba highlands. One of the southernmost Rhampholeon species. Named after James Marshall, a naturalist who collected specimens in southern Africa.

children.name: Rhampholeon boulengeri
children.description: An Albertine Rift species found in the montane forests of Rwanda, Burundi, eastern Democratic Republic of Congo, and southwestern Uganda. One of the larger Rhampholeon, reaching 9 cm. Named after the Belgian-British herpetologist George Albert Boulenger.

children.name: Rhampholeon nchisiensis
children.description: A small pygmy chameleon endemic to Malawi, primarily known from the Nchisi Mountain forest reserve. Inhabits the leaf litter of montane miombo woodland and forest edge. Its restricted range and small population make it susceptible to local extinction from habitat degradation.

children.name: Rhampholeon chapmanorum
children.description: Critically Endangered and known only from three small forest patches in the Thyolo and Mulanje highlands of southern Malawi. The forest has been almost entirely converted to tea estates, leaving only tiny remnant patches. One of the most threatened chameleon species in Africa.

children.name: Rhampholeon acuminatus
children.description: A distinctive leaf chameleon from the highlands of Tanzania; pointed snout for fossorial lifestyle.

children.name: Rhampholeon moyeri
children.description: A tiny species from the Udzungwa Mountains of Tanzania; one of the smallest chameleons.

children.name: Rhampholeon tilburyi
children.description: A tiny pygmy chameleon from the Eastern Arc Mountains of Tanzania.

children.name: Rhampholeon viridis
children.description: A small bright green species from the Usambara Mountains.

children.name: Rhampholeon marbarezi
children.description: A recently described pygmy chameleon from Mozambique; one of the smallest chameleons.

children.name: Rieppeleon
children.description: A small genus of three pygmy chameleons in East Africa, split from Rhampholeon in 2004. They inhabit the leaf litter and low shrubs of savanna woodland and forest edges, ranging from Kenya south through Tanzania to Mozambique. Smaller and more terrestrial than true chameleons, they rely on cryptic brown patterning and a swaying 'dead-leaf' walk for camouflage. Named in honour of herpetologist Olivier Rieppel.

children.name: Rieppeleon brevicaudatus
children.description: The most commonly kept species of Rieppeleon in captivity. Found in the forest edges and woodland of Kenya and Tanzania. The 'beard' refers to a small gular crest on the throat. Adults are 7–8 cm long and have a characteristically stubby tail, hence brevicaudatus (short-tailed).

children.name: Rieppeleon kerstenii
children.description: A small, terrestrial pygmy chameleon found in coastal Kenya, Tanzania, and Mozambique, particularly in sandy coastal forest and thicket. Named after Otto Kersten, a German explorer who joined Carl Klaus von der Decken's expeditions to East Africa in the 1860s.

children.name: Rieppeleon brachyurus
children.description: Distributed across savanna woodland in Kenya, Tanzania, and Uganda. Distinguished from R. brevicaudatus by scalation detail and a slightly larger overall size. Like its congeners, it is oviparous, laying small clutches of 2–6 eggs in shallow soil depressions.

children.name: Kinyongia
children.description: A genus of African chameleons split from Chamaeleo and Trioceros in 2006, concentrated in the montane forests of East Africa's Albertine Rift, Eastern Arc Mountains, and Ethiopian highlands. Most species are restricted to single mountain ranges, making them highly endemic and vulnerable to deforestation. Many have elaborate rostral appendages or casques. The genus is named from the Swahili word for chameleon.

children.name: Kinyongia fischeri
children.description: One of the most striking African chameleons, the males bearing a prominent, bifurcated nasal appendage. Restricted to montane forest remnants of the East Usambara Mountains in northeastern Tanzania. Named after the German naturalist and explorer Johann Gustav Fischer.

children.name: Kinyongia xenorhina
children.description: Found in the montane forests of the Rwenzori Mountains of Uganda and the eastern DRC, at elevations of 1,500–2,500 m. The common and scientific name both refer to the unusual compressed rostral appendage present in males. One of the emblematic chameleons of the Albertine Rift biodiversity hotspot.

children.name: Kinyongia tavetana
children.description: A small, two-horned chameleon restricted to the forested slopes of Mount Kilimanjaro and the Taita Hills of Kenya and Tanzania at elevations of 900–2,000 m. Males bear two small rostral horns. Threatened by loss and fragmentation of montane forest on Kilimanjaro's lower slopes.

children.name: Kinyongia tenuis
children.description: A slender, gracile chameleon from the Uluguru Mountains of Tanzania, one of the most biodiverse highland forests in Africa. Males lack prominent horns but have a compressed lateral casque. Highly dependent on intact Uluguru cloud forest habitat, where it is threatened by expanding agriculture.

children.name: Kinyongia matschiei
children.description: A medium-sized chameleon from montane forests of northeastern Tanzania and southeastern Kenya. Named after the German zoologist Paul Matschie, who described many East African vertebrate species. Males lack rostral horns but display vivid green and yellow colouration during interactions.

children.name: Kinyongia vosseleri
children.description: Restricted to the West Usambara Mountains of Tanzania, where it is found in humid montane forest at elevations above 1,400 m. Males bear two small rostral horns and display brilliant green colouration. Named after Julius Vosseler, a German zoologist who worked in East Africa.

children.name: Kinyongia multituberculata
children.description: Named for the unusually dense covering of conical tubercles across the body. Found in montane forests of the Nguru and Nguu Mountains of Tanzania. One of several range-restricted Kinyongia species whose survival is entirely dependent on the conservation of Tanzania's Eastern Arc Mountain forests.

children.name: Kinyongia carpenteri
children.description: An Albertine Rift species found in montane forests of Uganda and Rwanda above 2,000 m. Described by Parker in 1929 and named in honour of G.D. Carpenter, a British entomologist and naturalist who collected in East Africa. Males are brilliant green with blue and white lateral markings.

children.name: Kinyongia gyrosa
children.description: A small chameleon from the Kenyan highlands; males possess short curved horns.

children.name: Kinyongia ulugurensis
children.description: Endemic to the Uluguru Mountains of Tanzania; two parallel horns in males.

children.name: Nadzikambia
children.description: A small genus of montane chameleons restricted to the high mountain forests of Malawi and Mozambique, described in 2004 when split from Chamaeleo. The genus name derives from 'nadzikambia,' the Chichewa (Malawian) word for chameleon. These species inhabit isolated cloud forest patches on the summit ridges of the highest peaks in the region — Mulanje, Nyika, and Zomba — making them extremely range-restricted and vulnerable.

children.name: Nadzikambia mlanjensis
children.description: Restricted to the high plateau forests of Mount Mulanje in southern Malawi, above 1,800 m. One of the rarest and most geographically restricted chameleons in Africa. The summit plateau of Mulanje, southern Africa's highest mountain massif, is the sole known refuge for this species.

children.name: Nadzikambia baylissi
children.description: Described in 2010 from the Chiperone and Mabu forests of northern Mozambique, at elevations above 1,400 m. Named after Julian Bayliss, the British field biologist who led the expedition that discovered it. Known from extremely limited surveys; its true population size and range are still poorly understood.

children.name: Nadzikambia mlanjensis
children.description: Endemic to Mount Mulanje in Malawi; one of the rarest African chameleons.

children.name: Archaius
children.description: A monotypic genus of chameleon from the Seychelles; distinctive tiger-like stripes.

children.name: Archaius tigris
children.description: Endemic to the Seychelles; striking tiger-stripe pattern; threatened by habitat loss and invasive ants.

children.name: Bradypodion
children.description: Small, brightly coloured chameleons of southern Africa; give birth to live young.

children.name: Bradypodion pumilum
children.description: The most common dwarf chameleon in South Africa; variable colour from green to brown.

children.name: Bradypodion ventrale
children.description: A beautiful species from coastal forests of the Western Cape; bright green with yellow throat.

children.name: Bradypodion damaranum
children.description: A rare Namibian species; restricted to the Damaraland region; the most arid-adapted dwarf chameleon.

children.name: Bradypodion nemorale
children.description: A rare forest chameleon from KwaZulu-Natal; metallic green, electric blue and yellow.

children.name: Bradypodion montanum
children.description: A high-altitude species from the Drakensberg; adapted to cool mountain conditions.

children.name: Bradypodion melanocephalum
children.description: A small chameleon from KwaZulu-Natal; black head and bright green body.

children.name: Bradypodion damaranum
children.description: A colourful species from the Knysna forests; vivid green with a pale-blue throat.
